did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a novel machine learning method called Augmented May Fly optimized with K-Nearest Neighbors (AMFO-KNN) for skin cancer diagnosis. It integrates patient metadata with lesion images from datasets like PAD-UFES-20 and Fitzpatrick17k to classify benign versus malignant lesions, achieving 94.5% accuracy in the proposed model.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'CONFERENCE PAPER' and presents original experimental work. It introduces a novel machine learning method (AMFO-KNN), describes specific materials and methods (data collection from PAD-UFES-20, Fitzpatrick17k, and ISIC datasets), and reports original results including accuracy, precision, and recall metrics compared against baseline models (SVM, DCNN, RF).

title: Novel machine learning method in skin cancer diagnosis based on patient's metadata
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel machine learning method called Augmented May Fly optimized with K-Nearest Neighbors (AMFO-KNN) for skin cancer diagnosis by integrating patient metadata with lesion images. The researchers utilized data from the PAD-UFES-20, Fitzpatrick17k, and International Skin Imaging Collaboration datasets, which included 66,735 clinical photos reflecting 16 skin disease situations. Preprocessing involved removing noisy data with an Adaptive Median Filter and extracting features using Kernel Principal Component Analysis. The model was evaluated against traditional approaches like SVM, DCNN, and RF using accuracy, precision, F1-score, and recall metrics. The proposed AMFO-KNN method achieved a 94.5% accuracy in classifying benign versus malignant lesions, outperforming the baseline models which reported lower metrics such as 49% F1-score. The results demonstrate that incorporating patient information alongside image data significantly improves the performance of automated skin cancer diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Benign vs. Malignant skin cancer diagnosis
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between benign and malignant skin lesions. The model (AMFO-KNN) classifies samples based on patient metadata and lesion images. The study reports classification metrics including accuracy (48%), precision (45%), recall (47%), and F1-score (49%).

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing photographic samples of six distinct kinds of skin lesions, including patient metadata (gender, age, anatomical location) and biopsy status.

use.training: Used to train and assess the proposed AMFO-KNN system.
use.testing: Used to train and assess the proposed AMFO-KNN system.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Dermoscopic Archive
datasets_used.description: Comprises data from the 2019 and 2020 melanoma detection challenges. ISIC 2019 includes nine diagnostic categories. ISIC 2020 includes dermoscopic, biopsy-proven images labeled as malignant or benign from over 2,000 individuals, with metadata on gender, age, and anatomical location.

use.training: Used to train and assess the proposed AMFO-KNN system.
use.testing: Used to train and assess the proposed AMFO-KNN system.

datasets_used.name: Fitzpatrick17k
datasets_used.description: A dataset containing photographic images with a three-partition label for benign vs malignant lesions. It did not include patient metadata.

use.training: Used to train and assess the proposed AMFO-KNN system.
use.testing: Used to train and assess the proposed AMFO-KNN system.

datasets_used.name: Combined Custom Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset created by the authors from the three aforementioned databases, consisting of 66,735 clinical photos reflecting 16 distinct skin-disease situations (58,031 dermoscopy images and 8,704 photographs).

use.training: Used as the primary input for the proposed AMFO-KNN model.
use.testing: Used as the primary input for the proposed AMFO-KNN model.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and compared qualitatively against baseline models.

Combined Dataset (PAD-UFES-20, ISIC, Fit.description: Results reported for the combined dataset of 66,735 clinical photos (58,031 dermoscopy images and 8,704 photographs) reflecting 16 distinct skin-disease situations. The task was binary classification of benign vs. malignant lesions.
Combined Dataset (PAD-UFES-20, ISIC, Fit.notes: The paper states in the conclusion that the suggested technique was 94.5% accurate in identifying benign from malignant lesions, but the detailed quantitative results in Tables 1-4 report lower values (48% accuracy) for the specific comparison against baselines on the combined dataset. The 94.5% figure appears to be a summary claim or potentially refers to a different subset/evaluation not detailed in the tables, while the tables provide the specific comparative metrics.

architecture_used.main_model: Augmented May Fly optimized with K-Nearest Neighbors (AMFO-KNN)

architecture_details.description: A hybrid model combining an optimization algorithm (Augmented May Fly Optimization) with a K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) classifier. The AMFO algorithm is used to improve the classification efficiency of KNN. The system integrates patient metadata (structured data) with lesion images (unstructured data).
architecture_details.preprocessing: Adaptive Median Filter (AMF) for noise removal; Kernel Principal Component Analysis (KPCA) for feature extraction from segmented data.
architecture_details.activation_function: Swish activation function (mentioned in conclusion as part of the KNN classification stage improvement)
architecture_details.convolution_type: Depth-wise separable convolutions (mentioned in conclusion as part of the KNN classification stage improvement)

hyperparameters.k_value_selection: Chosen during cross-validation to mitigate noise effects

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Augmented May Fly optimized with K-Nearest Neighbors (AMFO-KNN)
preprocessing_techniques: Adaptive Median Filter (AMF) for removing impulse noise from raw data samples
Kernel Principal Component Analysis (KPCA) for feature extraction from segmented data

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Mentioned in the conclusion as a potential area for future research to investigate its effect on CNN performance; not applied in the current study.
Used by Babu and Peter (2021) to balance class distribution; cited as a related method but not used in the proposed AMFO-KNN model.

conclusions: The proposed Augmented May Fly optimized with K-Nearest Neighbors (AMFO-KNN) method outperforms traditional approaches (SVM, DCNN, and RF) in terms of accuracy, precision, F1-score, and recall for skin cancer diagnosis.
The AMFO-KNN model achieved an accuracy of 48%, precision of 45%, recall of 47%, and F1-score of 49% on the combined dataset.
Incorporating patient metadata (age, gender, anatomical location) alongside lesion images improves the performance of automated skin cancer diagnosis compared to using image data alone.

The study acknowledges that while deep learning models like CNNs offer high accuracy, they require sophisticated computing infrastructure that is impractical for rural and low-resource healthcare settings, highlighting the potential advantage of the proposed method in such contexts.
The authors suggest that further research is needed to investigate the effect of data augmentation techniques, such as Cutout regularization, on CNN performance.
important_remarks: The reported accuracy of the proposed AMFO-KNN model is 48%, which is substantially lower than the 94.5% accuracy claimed in the abstract and conclusion; this discrepancy suggests a potential reporting error or inconsistency in the paper.

The study lacks external validation, relying solely on a combined dataset derived from PAD-UFES-20, ISIC, and Fitzpatrick17k without specifying train/validation/test splits or cross-validation protocols for the final evaluation.
The paper does not report standard deviation or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, limiting the assessment of result stability and statistical significance.

The dataset construction involves merging images from multiple sources (dermoscopy and photographic) with varying metadata availability (Fitzpatrick17k lacked metadata), which may introduce heterogeneity and bias not fully addressed.

The comparison baselines (SVM, DCNN, RF) are cited from other studies (Babu and Peter 2021, Saba et al 2019, Luu et al 2021) rather than re-implemented and trained on the same data split, making direct performance comparison potentially invalid due to differing data distributions and preprocessing.
The paper does not disclose the specific value of 'k' used in the KNN classifier, only stating it was chosen during cross-validation, which hinders reproducibility.

The study acknowledges that deep learning models require sophisticated computing infrastructure impractical for low-resource settings, but the proposed AMFO-KNN method's computational complexity and inference speed are not quantified.
No statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the superiority of the proposed method over baselines.
